old_claim.note: Early Firearms is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Fourteenth-century hand cannons and harquebuses form the firearm lineage that true flintlock lockwork later improves, but they are not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica's advent-of-firearms section places firearms in Europe in the fourteenth century and harquebuses in the fifteenth century, before the ca. 1620 French flintlock evidence used for this node.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source anchors a pre-flintlock European firearm sequence from early firearms to harquebuses.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a historical-predecessor edge rather than a required-method edge because the predecessor source establishes earlier firearm lineage while the node-specific sources establish the later flintlock mechanism.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: early_firearms remains the historical predecessor edge for muskets_flintlock, now with source-backed lineage evidence.
would_reject_if: The early_firearms node were narrowed to a non-firearm technology outside the lineage of muskets.
A stronger source showed true flintlocks arose independently of earlier handheld firearm traditions.
The muskets_flintlock node were rescoped away from firearms and only represented a non-weapon ignition mechanism.
short_reason: Early hand firearms are a sourced lineage predecessor to flintlock muskets.
